description:
You are a junior backup-operator who has just received a compressed
    configuration backup created by the previous shift.
    Your job is to prove that the data can actually be restored,
    and to leave an auditable trail that automated tools can re-check.

    1.  A single archive already exists on the machine at

            /home/user/backups/site_config_backup.tar.gz

        The archive was produced with “tar –czf” from the machine’s root,
        so the paths inside the archive start with  
        “etc/nginx/ …” and “var/www/html/ …”.  
        Do NOT try to extract anything into “/etc” or “/var”; you do not
        have elevated privileges.  Instead, do everything inside the user’s
        home directory.

    2.  Create a brand new directory that will act as the temporary
        restore target.  The directory must be

            /home/user/restore_test

        If it already exists, remove it first so that the test
        starts from a clean slate.

    3.  Restore the archive into that directory, recreating the directory
        hierarchy exactly as it appears in the archive.  After the restore,
        the following five files must be present relative to
        “/home/user/restore_test”:

                etc/nginx/nginx.conf
                etc/nginx/conf.d/default.conf
                var/www/html/index.html
                var/www/html/healthcheck.php
                var/www/html/assets/style.css

    4.  Produce a **restore log** called

            /home/user/restore_test/restore_report.log

        one line per file **in the exact order shown above**.  
        The file must be plain ASCII text and must end with a single
        trailing newline.  Every line must be formatted exactly like this:

                <relative_path>|<size_bytes>|<md5sum>

        • <relative_path>  – the path **relative to /home/user/restore_test**
          (no leading slash).  
        • <size_bytes>     – file size in bytes, as given by
          “stat --format=%s” (i.e. an unsigned integer with no units).  
        • <md5sum>         – the 32-character hexadecimal MD-5 digest of the
          file’s current contents, as produced by “md5sum”.

        Example (do **NOT** copy this literally):
                some/path/to/file.txt|42|a1b2c3d4e5f60718293a4b5c6d7e8f90

        The automated checker will reject any deviation in order, delimiter,
        capitalisation, or whitespace.

    5.  When you are finished, print the contents of
        “/home/user/restore_test/restore_report.log” to STDOUT
        so the training staff can see the result immediately.

    The whole job can be done with 5–10 standard command-line utilities
    (mkdir, rm, tar, md5sum, stat, printf, cat, etc.).
    Do NOT run any command that requires root privileges.
truth:
The container already contains the compressed archive
    “/home/user/backups/site_config_backup.tar.gz”.
    Inside that archive the following files exist with these properties
    (all paths are exactly as they appear *inside the archive*,
    i.e. without a leading slash):

        1. etc/nginx/nginx.conf
           ─ content: “abcdefghijklmnopqrstuvwxyz”
           ─ size:    26 bytes
           ─ md5sum:  c3fcd3d76192e4007dfb496cca67e13b

        2. etc/nginx/conf.d/default.conf
           ─ content: “abc”
           ─ size:    3 bytes
           ─ md5sum:  900150983cd24fb0d6963f7d28e17f72

        3. var/www/html/index.html
           ─ content: “a”
           ─ size:    1 byte
           ─ md5sum:  0cc175b9c0f1b6a831c399e269772661

        4. var/www/html/healthcheck.php
           ─ content: “”   (empty file)
           ─ size:    0 bytes
           ─ md5sum:  d41d8cd98f00b204e9800998ecf8427e

        5. var/www/html/assets/style.css
           ─ content: “message digest”
           ─ size:    14 bytes
           ─ md5sum:  f96b697d7cb7938d525a2f31aaf161d0

    Therefore, after a correct restore the file

        /home/user/restore_test/restore_report.log

    must contain **exactly** the following five lines
    (with a single “\n” character after the last line):

        etc/nginx/nginx.conf|26|c3fcd3d76192e4007dfb496cca67e13b
        etc/nginx/conf.d/default.conf|3|900150983cd24fb0d6963f7d28e17f72
        var/www/html/index.html|1|0cc175b9c0f1b6a831c399e269772661
        var/www/html/healthcheck.php|0|d41d8cd98f00b204e9800998ecf8427e
        var/www/html/assets/style.css|14|f96b697d7cb7938d525a2f31aaf161d0

    The automated test will:

        • Ensure “/home/user/restore_test” exists and has the five files.
        • Read “restore_report.log”, verify it contains exactly five lines,
          in the exact order and with the exact MD-5 sums given above.
name: task_000000_a1dca837
